text.title: GraphQL Schema Design Patterns
text.summary: Schema-first design: scalars, interfaces, Relay connections, input types, mutation payloads with typed errors.

rule.statement: Treat `schema.graphql` (SDL) as the source of truth. Run codegen in CI and fail if generated files drift from the committed schema.
rule.rationale: SDL-first ensures the API contract is reviewed before any implementation. Committing generated files by hand allows them to diverge from the schema silently.

rule.statement: Every root type should implement `Node` (with `id: ID!`) and `Timestamped` (with `createdAt`, `updatedAt`). This enables consistent caching and relay compliance.
rule.rationale: Relay client caching is keyed by `id`. Without a consistent `id` field, clients cannot normalize the cache. Timestamps are universally needed for auditing and display.

rule.statement: Every mutation must return a typed payload: `MutationNamePayload` containing the result object and a `userErrors: [Error!]!` array. Never throw exceptions for business errors.
rule.rationale: HTTP always returns 200 for GraphQL. Exceptions become opaque `errors[]` entries. Typed payloads allow clients to handle business errors (validation, conflicts) structurally in the same type system as success responses.

section.title: Relay Connections

rule.statement: Use Relay cursor connections for every list field that could grow beyond 10 items. Never expose offset/limit pagination in the public schema.
rule.rationale: Offset pagination is unstable when items are inserted or deleted mid-page. Cursor pagination returns consistent results regardless of concurrent modifications. Relay clients (and many non-Relay clients) depend on the `PageInfo` structure for infinite scroll.

section.reference: Full reference schema with Connection types and mutation payloads
